text: The surgeon begins with a peritoneal flap and proceeds with a lateral first dissection due to the presence of a reservoir. The dissection occurs in the pre-transversalis plane, transitioning to a pre-peritoneal dissection towards the indirect hernia sac. The moderate-sized inguinal scrotal hernia is reduced, revealing the epigastric vessels medially. A larger lateral pocket is developed to accommodate an extra-large mesh, while the medial dissection reveals scarring around the reservoir, appearing to adhere to the transversalis fascia.

The surgeon carefully dissects the initial scarring layer, revealing the encapsulated plastic reservoir. As the capsule is preserved, a small amount of serous fluid is released upon entry. The dissection progresses to the retropubic area, focusing around the obturator and vas deferens, establishing a critical view of safety for the myopectenial orifice.

The surgeon reviews the anatomy, showing the midline reservoir tubing, Cooper's ligament, femoral space, indirect space, and surrounding vessels. The reservoir is positioned outside its initial capsule, tracked over the pubis. An extra-large heavyweight mesh with a two-centimeter slit is placed to accommodate contralateral coverage, ensuring appropriate coverage of the myopectineal orifice.
The surgeon secures the mesh to the scar tissue instead of Cooper's ligament. The peritoneal flap is closed in a standard fashion, with no major intraoperative issues observed.
